skillName: Solve problems involving tables and timetables
misconceptionSummary: Students calculate journey time by subtracting hours and minutes independently, ignoring borrowing across the hour boundary.

lines.text: Count on from departure to arrival

steps.narration: Read the departure and arrival times from the timetable. To find journey duration, count forward from the departure time to the arrival time — do not just subtract the digits.

Count on in stages: from nine forty-seven to ten o'clock is thirteen minutes; from ten to eleven is sixty minutes; from eleven to eleven twenty-three is twenty-three minutes. Total: ninety-six minutes, which is one hour thirty-six minutes.

To find arrival time, add the duration in stages. Add the minutes first: thirty-five plus forty-eight is eighty-three minutes — sixty makes one hour, leaving twenty-three minutes, so fifteen twenty-three. Then add the hour: sixteen twenty-three.

visuals.label: Count on in stages: to the hour, through full hours, to arrival

steps.narration: One hour thirty-six minutes. Counting on in stages avoids the borrowing errors that come from subtracting directly.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: 11:23 − 09:47 → 11−9=2 hours, 23−47 impossible → error. Count on instead: 09:47 → 10:00 (13 min) → 11:00 (60 min) → 11:23 (23 min) = 96 min.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Never subtract hours and minutes independently — minutes don't go up to 100. Count forward from departure in stages: to the next hour, through full hours, to arrival.
